title: Mule Suttles
diff_added: In 2024, Suttles was officially recognized as the 4th greatest all-time slugger when the Negro League statistics were incorporated into the MLB records, far surpassing Mark McGwire and also relegating Barry Bonds to 8th place.
diff_removed: In 2024, Suttles was officially recognized as the 4th greatest all-time slugger when the Negro League statistics were incorporated into the MLB records, far surpassing Mark Mcguire and also relegating Barry Bonds to 8th place.

title: Dravidian languages
diff_added: The '''Dravidian languages''' are a [[language family|family of languages]] spoken by 250 million people, primarily in [[South India]], parts of [[North India]], [[Bangladesh]] and [[Nepal]], north-east [[Sri Lanka]], and south-west [[Pakistan]], with pockets in [[Afghanistan]] and [[Iran]].{{sfnp|Steever|2020|p=1}}{{sfnp|Kolichala|2016|p=76}}
diff_removed: The '''Dravidian languages''' are a [[language family|family of languages]] spoken by 250 million people, primarily in [[South India]], north-east [[Sri Lanka]], and south-west [[Pakistan]], with pockets in [[Afghanistan]] and [[Iran]].{{sfnp|Steever|2020|p=1}}{{sfnp|Kolichala|2016|p=76}}

title: Quintus Glitius Atilius Agricola
diff_added: '''Quintus Glitius Atilius Agricola''' was a [[Roman Empire|Roman]] [[Roman Senate|senator]] and general who held several posts in the emperor's service. He was twice [[Roman consul|suffect consul]]: for the first time in AD 97 with [[Lucius Pomponius Sura]]<ref>All that survives of the consul's name on the ''Fasti ostienses'' is "..]us", which Zevi had plausibly restored as [[Lucius Licinius Sura]]. However, two more recently recovered fragments of military diplomas show that the name of this consul is L. Pomponius Maternus, who is otherwise unknown. (Eck and Pangerl, [https://www.jstor.org/stable/20191986 "Zwei Konstitutionen für die Truppen Niedermösiens vom 9. September 97"], ''[[Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik]]'', 151 (2005), pp. 185-192</ref> as his colleague,<ref>Fausto Zevi, [https://www.jstor.org/stable/23459091 "I consoli del 97 d. Cr. in due framenti gia' editi dei Fasti Ostienses"], ''Listy filologické / Folia philologica'', 96 (1973), pp. 125-137</ref> and the second time in 103 when he replaced the emperor [[Trajan]].<ref>''[[Fasti Ostienses]]'', edited Ladislav Vidman (Prague: Acadamia, 1982), frag. Gd l. 6</ref> He is the last known person to have held two suffect consulates.<ref>Such iterated consulates are discussed by [[Ronald Syme]], [https://www.jstor.org/stable/297794 review of ''I Fasti Consolari dell' Impero Romano dal 30 Av anti Christo al 613 Dopo Christo'' by Attilio Degrassi], ''[[Journal of Roman Studies]]'', 43 (1953), pp. 148-161</ref> Agricola is known only through a large number of fragmentary inscriptions from [[Augusta Taurinorum]], which appears to be his home town.

However, two more recently recovered fragments of military diplomas show that the name of this consul is L. Pomponius Maternus, who is otherwise unknown. September 97"], ''[[Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik]]'', 151 (2005), pp. 185-192</ref> as his colleague,<ref>Fausto Zevi, [https://www.jstor.org/stable/23459091 "I consoli del 97 d. Gd l. 6</ref> He is the last known person to have held two suffect consulates.<ref>Such iterated consulates are discussed by [[Ronald Syme]], [https://www.jstor.org/stable/297794 review of ''I Fasti Consolari dell' Impero Romano dal 30 Av anti Christo al 613 Dopo Christo'' by Attilio Degrassi], ''[[Journal of Roman Studies]]'', 43 (1953), pp. 148-161</ref> Agricola is known only through a large number of fragmentary inscriptions from [[Augusta Taurinorum]], which appears to be his home town.

title: List of shipwrecks in March 1942
diff_added: |desc=World War II: The MO-4 Type [[minesweeper]] ({{GRT|50|disp=}}) was set on fire by enemy artillery fire while being moored in Streletskaya Bay near [[Sevastopol]]. Two of her crew and one of SKA-0183 braved the fire to throw in the sea the depht charges and ammunition aboard and were killed when the ship finally exploded. Their sacrifice avoided a far bigger and deadlier explosion. One, senior sailor [[Ivan Golubets]] of SKA-0183, was awarded the title of [[Hero of the Soviet Union]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://russ-flot.narod.ru/x-0014_MO-IV.htm |title=MO-4 type |publisher=russ-flot.narod.ru |access-date=25 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://flot.com/history/events/golubetz.htm |title=Ivan Golubets |publisher=flot.com |access-date=25 March 2026}}</ref>

title: Glossary of solitaire terms
diff_added: Games of [[solitaire]], or patience, have their own 'language' of specialised terms such as [[#building down|"building down"]], [[#packing|"packing"]], [[#foundations|"foundations"]], [[#talon|"talon"]] and [[#tableau|"tableau"]].  Once learnt they are helpful in describing, succinctly and accurately, how the games are played. Solitaire games are usually for a single player, although a small number have been designed for two and, in rare cases, three or even four players. They are games of skill or chance or a combination of the two. There are three classes of solitaire grouped by object.<ref name=Hoffmann/>
diff_removed: Games of [[solitaire]], or patience, have their own 'language' of specialised terms such as [[#building down|"building down"]], [[#packing|"packing"]], [[#foundations|"foundations"]], [[#talon|"talon"]] and [[#tableau|"tableau"]].  Once learnt they are helpful in describing, succinctly and accurately, how the games are played. Patience games are usually for a single player, although a small number have been designed for two and, in rare cases, three or even four players. They are games of skill or chance or a combination of the two. There are three classes of patience grouped by object.<ref name=Hoffmann/>

title: George Brown (cricketer, born 1887)
diff_added: '''George Brown''' (6 October 1887 – 3 December 1964) was an English professional [[cricket]]er who played in seven [[Test cricket|Test matches]] between 1921 and 1923 and in English [[county cricket]] with [[Hampshire County Cricket Club|Hampshire]] from 1908 to 1933. Brown was born in [[Cowley, Oxford|Cowley]] and made his debut in [[first-class cricket]] for Hampshire in 1908. He soon established himself as one of the finest [[all-rounder]]s in county cricket. After strong performances for Hampshire following the [[First World War]], Brown made his Test debut for [[England cricket team|England]] against [[Australia national cricket team|Australia]] in the [[Australian cricket team in England in 1921|1921 Ashes series]]. Despite not being Hampshire's regular [[wicket-keeper]], he was chosen in the England side to fulfil this role. He performed well against the Australian fast bowlers [[Jack Gregory (cricketer)|Jack Gregory]] and [[Ted McDonald]] and was described by the cricket writer [[A. A. Thomson]] as "one of the few English heroes of the ill-starred 1921 Tests". He [[English cricket team in South Africa in 1922–23|toured South Africa in 1922–23]], playing in four of the five Tests to conclude his brief Test career. Brown continued to play county cricket until 1933, when injury forced him to retire. He then spent two seasons on the first-class [[umpire (cricket)|umpires]] list.

title: Gaius Curtius Justus
diff_added: '''Gaius Curtius Justus''' was a [[Roman Empire|Roman]] [[Roman Senate|senator]] who held several posts in the emperor's service during the [[Antonine dynasty]]. He was [[roman consul|suffect consul]] in 150 with [[Gaius Julius Julianus]] as his colleague.<ref>Roger S. O. Tomlin, John Pearce, [https://www.academia.edu/42723314/A_ROMAN_MILITARY_DIPLOMA_FOR_THE_GERMAN_FLEET_19_NOVEMBER_150_FOUND_IN_NORTHERN_BRITAIN "A Roman Military Diploma for the German Fleet (19 November 150) Found in Northern Britain"], ''[[Zeitschrift für Papyrologie und Epigraphik]]'', 206 (2018), pp. 214–216</ref> Justus is known primarily through surviving inscriptions, although he could be identical with the Curtius Justus mentioned as a ''scriptor rei rusticae'' by [[Gargilius Martialis]] (2.1.4,7).<ref name=Birley-253>Anthony R. Birley, ''The Fasti of Roman Britain'', (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1981), p. 253</ref>

diff_added: '''Lusatia''',{{efn|English: {{IPAc-en|l|uː|ˈ|s|eɪ|ʃ|(|i|)|ə}} {{respell|loo|SAY|sh(ee|)ə}}; {{Langx|de|Lausitz}} {{IPA|de|ˈlaʊzɪts||De-Lausitz.ogg}}; {{langx|pl|Łużyce}} {{IPA|pl|wuˈʐɨt͡sɛ||Pl-Łużyce.ogg}}; {{langx|hsb|Łužica}} {{IPA|hsb|ˈwuʒitsa|}}; {{langx|dsb|Łužyca}} {{IPA|dsb|ˈwuʒɨtsa|}}; {{langx|cs|Lužice}} {{IPA|cs|ˈluʒɪtsɛ|}}.}} otherwise known as '''Sorbia''', is a region in [[Central Europe]], territorially split between [[Germany]] and [[Poland]]. Lusatia stretches from the [[Bóbr]] and [[Kwisa]] rivers in the east to the [[Pulsnitz (river)|Pulsnitz]] and [[Black Elster]] rivers in the west, and is located within the German states of [[Saxony]] and [[Brandenburg]] and the Polish [[voivodeship]]s of [[Lower Silesian Voivodeship|Lower Silesia]] and [[Lubusz Voivodeship|Lubusz]]. Major rivers of Lusatia are the [[Spree (river)|Spree]] and the [[Lusatian Neisse]], which defines the border between Germany and Poland. The [[Lusatian Mountains]] of the [[Sudetes|Western Sudetes]] separate Lusatia from [[Bohemia]] ([[Czech Republic]]) in the south. Lusatia is traditionally divided into [[Upper Lusatia]], the hilly southern part, and [[Lower Lusatia]], the flat northern part.

title: Gaggenau
diff_added:
In 1691, the area was at the heart of the [[Nine Years' War|Palatinate War of Succession]] and was almost completely destroyed by the French forces. In 1772, Anton Rindeschwender established a successful glassworks factory below the village, and along with it a number of new residences and supporting businesses in the area.
The real industrial boom began in 1873 with the establishment of the Michael Flürscheims Ironworks, which resurrected an iron smelter originally established by the [[Margrave of Baden]] in the late 18th century. In 1895, the factory built the 5-hp automobile ''Orient Express'' and entered the new industry of automobile manufacture.  In 1905 they renamed themselves the [[Gaggenau South German Automobile Factory GmbH]]. In 1907 the company was taken over by the company [[Benz & Cie]] of Mannheim until the merger of [[Daimler-Benz]] AG in 1926.

title: British Colonial Auxiliary Forces
diff_added: The '''British Colonial Auxiliary Forces''' were the various military forces (each composed of one or more units or corps) of [[British Empire|Britain's colonial empire]] which were not considered part of the [[British Army]] proper. Most were part-time units, and they were the colonial equivalents of the British "Home" Auxiliary Forces. Both Colonial and Home Auxiliary Forces were originally referred to as "Reserve Forces" (until the formation of the [[Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)#Army Reserve (Regular)|Regular Reserve of the British Army]] caused confusion) and (due to their being tasked purely with home defence, so that their personnel could not be sent overseas on expeditionary service unless they specially volunteered for this) as "Local Forces" and "Home Defence Forces" or "Defence Forces". A small number of colonial reserve units (such as the Militia and Volunteer units of [[Bermuda]]) were funded by the war Office as parts of the British Army. A small number of regular military units (such as the [[Royal West African Frontier Force]]) were also raised in the colonies and not funded by the War Office (and were organised under the [[Colonial Office]]), and these were also considered auxiliary to the British Army. Other regular colonial units, notably the [[West India Regiments|West India Regiment]], were funded by the War Office as parts of the British Army.

Both Colonial and Home Auxiliary Forces were originally referred to as "Reserve Forces" (until the formation of the [[Regular Reserve (United Kingdom)#Army Reserve (Regular)|Regular Reserve of the British Army]] caused confusion) and (due to their being tasked purely with home defence, so that their personnel could not be sent overseas on expeditionary service unless they specially volunteered for this) as "Local Forces" and "Home Defence Forces" or "Defence Forces". Other regular colonial units, notably the [[West India Regiments|West India Regiment]], were funded by the War Office as parts of the British Army.

title: Marek Heinz
diff_added:
Heinz headed to German side [[Hamburger SV]] in 2000.<ref name="Hrabě"/>{{rp|p=527}} A surplus to requirements under head coach [[Kurt Jara]] midway through the 2002–03 season, he joined [[Arminia Bielefeld]] for the remainder of the campaign with an option to make the move permanent if Bielefeld avoided relegation.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Goss |first1=Patrick |title=Bielefeld snap up Heinz |url=https://www.skysports.com/football/news/2254996/bielefeld-snap-up-heinz |access-date=2026-03-20 |work=Sky Sports |date=2003-01-16}}</ref> Bielefeld were relegated from the [[Bundesliga]] after losing their final game of the season in May 2003.<ref>{{cite news |title=Erst schlapp, dann tot |url=https://www.spiegel.de/sport/fussball/bye-bye-bielefeld-erst-schlapp-dann-tot-a-250240.html |access-date=2026-03-20 |work=[[Der Spiegel (website)|Der Spiegel]] |date=2003-05-25 |language=de}}</ref>
[[FC Baník Ostrava|Baník Ostrava]] signed Heinz from Hamburg ahead of the 2003–04 season.<ref>{{cite news |last=Procházka |first=David |title=Před 18 lety získal Baník Ostrava zatím poslední mistrovský titul v historii klubu |trans-title=18 years ago Baník Ostrava won the last championship title in the club's history |url=https://ostrava.rozhlas.cz/100-let-baniku-ostrava-serial-o-historii-slavnych-momentech-a-legendach-8733175/13 |access-date=2026-03-25 |work=Czech Radio |date=2022-07-21 |language=cs}}</ref> He made his debut for the club in July 2003, providing two [[assist (association football)|assists]] in a win against [[SK Dynamo České Budějovice|České Budějovice]].<ref>{{Cite news|title=Heinz v premiéře na góly jen přihrával|trans-title=Heinz only assisted goals on his debut|url=https://www.idnes.cz/fotbal/prvni-liga/heinz-v-premiere-na-goly-jen-prihraval.A030728_212209_fotbal_jam|work=iDNES.cz|date=2003-07-29|access-date=2026-03-25|language=cs}}</ref> Heinz scored his first Baník goal in a 1–1 draw against [[FC Slovan Liberec|Liberec]] just a week into the season.<ref name="Hrabě"/>{{rp|p=528}} The player led Baník to the Czech title in the [[2003–04 Czech First League]] and finished the season as the league's top goalscorer with 19 goals,<ref name="2021int">{{Cite news|first=Jan|last=Dočkal|title=Heinz: Do semifinále s Řeckem jsem nenastoupil kvůli Beránkovi. A řekl jsem mu to|url=https://www.idnes.cz/fotbal/reprezentace/z-voleje-podcast-marek-heinz-euro-2004-zpoved-miroslav-beranek.A211107_103218_fot_reprez_tof|work=iDNES.cz|date=2021-11-08|access-date=2026-03-18|language=cs}}</ref> forming a partnership with [[Miroslav Matušovič]] which returned 29 goals for the club that season.<ref name="04squad">{{Cite news|title=Czechs drop Matusovic|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/euro_2004/czech_rep/3727921.stm|work=BBC Sport|date=2004-06-03|access-date=2026-03-18}}</ref>

title: Two Cannot be Denied
diff_added: The '''Two Cannot be Denied''' ({{lang-zh|s=两个不能否定|hp=Liǎng gè bùnéng fǒudìng}}), also written as '''two cannot negate''', is a political concept and slogan first proposed by [[Chinese Communist Party]] (CCP) [[General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party|general secretary]] [[Xi Jinping]] in January 2013. It refers to the phrase "the historical period after [[reform and opening up]] cannot be used to negate the historical period before reform and opening up, nor can the historical period before reform and opening up be used to negate the historical period after reform and opening up".
diff_removed: The '''Two Cannot be Denied''' ({{lang-zh|s=两个不能否定|hp=Liǎng gè bùnéng fǒudìng}}) is a political concept and slogan first proposed by [[Chinese Communist Party]] (CCP) [[General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party|general secretary]] [[Xi Jinping]] in January 2013. It refers to the phrase "the historical period after [[reform and opening up]] cannot be used to negate the historical period before reform and opening up, nor can the historical period before reform and opening up be used to negate the historical period after reform and opening up".

title: World Vitiligo Day
comment: Inactive links replaced and removed, citations added, WHA May resolution corrected to reflect the correct resolution as EB156(24) pertains to a different session on air pollution, citations added for the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology, specific EU parliamentary session replaced to reflect their broader advocacy work, and more closely reflect the available sources
diff_added:
The campaign was initially sparked by Ogo Maduewesi, founder and Executive Director of the Vitiligo Support and Awareness Foundation (VITSAF), who organized “Vitiligo Purple Fun Day” in Lagos, Nigeria, in 2011. The event honored the memory of [[Michael Jackson]] — who publicly lived with vitiligo until his death on June 25, 2009 — and brought together dermatologists, artists, motivational speakers, and patients to promote understanding of the condition. While not originally named as such, the event was later recognized as a precursor to World Vitiligo Day.
The initiative was further developed and formalized by the [[Vitiligo Research Foundation|Vitiligo Research Foundation (VRF)]], led by Yan Valle. In 2012, the VRF partnered with VITSAF and other organizations to coordinate global events and advocacy efforts, including a petition to the United Nations seeking official recognition of the day.{{Infobox holiday
Following the inaugural event, the VRF assumed a leading role in organizing and promoting World Vitiligo Day globally. The campaign grew rapidly, in part due to the active support of the global vitiligo community and public figures connected to Michael Jackson's legacy. A new petition drive eventually surpassed 500,000 signatures by mid-2015.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Valle |first=Y. |last2=Arenas Soto |first2=C. M. |last3=Kassymkhanova |first3=A. A. |last4=Ocampo-Candiani |first4=J. |last5=Gondokaryono |first5=S. P. |last6=Binić |first6=I. |last7=Thuong |first7=N. V. |last8=Cunha |first8=P. R. |last9=Hercogová |first9=J. |last10=Gao |first10=X. H. |last11=Parsad |first11=D. |last12=Lim |first12=H. W. |last13=Lotti |first13=T. |last14=Sigova |first14=J. |date=2025 |title=World vitiligo day: a model for grassroots medical activism and pharmaceutical innovation |url=https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12037365/ |journal=Frontiers in Medicine |volume=12 |pages=1571422 |doi=10.3389/fmed.2025.1571422 |issn=2296-858X |pmc=12037365 |pmid=40303368}}</ref>
The [[World Health Assembly]]'s May 2025 resolution WHA78.15 agenda item 13.4, which frames skin diseases as a global public-health priority, gave new momentum to dermatology advocacy across Europe.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 May 2025 |others=Minutes of the Seventy-eighth World Health Assembly |title=Skin diseases as a global public health priority |url=https://apps.who.int/gb/ebwha/pdf_files/WHA78/A78_R15-en.pdf |url-status=live |access-date=25 March 2026 |website=World Health Organisation}}</ref> The European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology urged institutions to reflect the resolution <ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-05-27 |title=EADV Welcomes the Resolution For Skin Diseases - EADV |url=https://eadv.org/2025/05/27/eadv-welcomes-the-resolution-for-skin-diseases/ |access-date=2026-03-25 |website=eadv.org |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Member States Adopt Landmark Resolution on Skin Diseases at 78th World Health Assembly |url=https://globalskin.org/component/content/article/78-gs-2
diff_removed:
The campaign was initially sparked by Ogo Maduewesi, founder and Executive Director of the [[Vitiligo Support and Awareness Foundation (VITSAF)]], who organized “Vitiligo Purple Fun Day” in Lagos, Nigeria, in 2011. The event honored the memory of [[Michael Jackson]] — who publicly lived with vitiligo until his death on June 25, 2009 — and brought together dermatologists, artists, motivational speakers, and patients to promote understanding of the condition. While not originally named as such, the event was later recognized as a precursor to World Vitiligo Day.
The initiative was further developed and formalized by the [[Vitiligo Research Foundation (VRF)]], led by Yan Valle. In 2012, the VRF partnered with VITSAF and other organizations to coordinate global events and advocacy efforts, including a petition to the United Nations seeking official recognition of the day.{{Infobox holiday
Following the inaugural event, the VRF assumed a leading role in organizing and promoting World Vitiligo Day globally. The campaign grew rapidly, in part due to the active support of the global vitiligo community and public figures connected to Michael Jackson's legacy. A new petition drive eventually surpassed 500,000 signatures by mid-2015.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}}
The [[World Health Assembly]]'s May 2025 resolution EB156(24), which frames skin diseases as a global public-health priority, gave new momentum to dermatology advocacy across Europe. The European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology urged EU institutions to reflect the resolution in forthcoming EU4 Health and Horizon Europe work programmes.

title: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
comment: /* After the second world war: Europe */ rm link to Security Council Resolution 428, which was from 1978; this is a reference to General Assembly Resolution 428 from 1950
diff_added: UNHCR's mandate was originally set out in its statute, annexed to Resolution 428(V) of the General Assembly of 1950. According to UNHCR, its mandate is to provide, on a non-political and humanitarian basis, international protection to refugees and to seek permanent solutions for them.<ref name="unhcr.org" /> This mandate has been subsequently broadened by numerous resolutions of the General Assembly and its [[United Nations Economic and Social Council|Economic and Social Council]] (ECOSOC).<ref name="unhcr.org"/> People who were already receiving assistance from other organs of the United Nations, such as [[UNKRA]] and [[UNRWA]], were excluded from UNHCR’s mandate.<ref name="unhcr.org"/>
diff_removed: UNHCR's mandate was originally set out in its statute, annexed to [[United Nations Security Council Resolution 428|resolution 428]] (V) of the General Assembly of 1950. According to UNHCR, its mandate is to provide, on a non-political and humanitarian basis, international protection to refugees and to seek permanent solutions for them.<ref name="unhcr.org" /> This mandate has been subsequently broadened by numerous resolutions of the General Assembly and its [[United Nations Economic and Social Council|Economic and Social Council]] (ECOSOC).<ref name="unhcr.org"/> People who were already receiving assistance from other organs of the United Nations, such as [[UNKRA]] and [[UNRWA]], were excluded from UNHCR’s mandate.<ref name="unhcr.org"/>

title: Lenka Reinerová
diff_added:
The couple returned to [[Czechoslovakia]] after 1948. In the 1950s, Reinerová was jailed by Czechoslovak [[communist]] authorities and spent 15 months in prison; she recorded this experience in one of her novels, ''[[Alle Farben der Sonne und der Nacht]]''. After her release she published sporadically. In 1958, she became the Editor of the German language publication 'Im Herzen Europas'<ref>Hélène Leclerc, Lenka Reinerová und die Zeitschrift "Im Herzen Europas". Internationale Kulturbeziehungen während des Prager Frühlings, Vienna, Cologne, Böhlau, 2022, ISBN 978-3-412-52538-5, p. 393</ref> (In the Heart of Europe) published by the Orbis Publishing House in Prague; in October 1968 she became the Editor in Chief. From the summer of 1968, and throughout the period following the Warsaw Pact invasion, Im Herzen Europas continued to support the Action Programme of the Communist Party. She remained as Editor in Chief until at least the end of 1969. Later she was not allowed to publish at all until the fall of [[communism]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2016-05-17 |title=Zum 100. Geburtstag der deutschsprachigen Pragerin Lenka Reinerová |url=https://deutsch.radio.cz/zum-100-geburtstag-der-deutschsprachigen-pragerin-lenka-reinerova-8225529 |access-date=2024-10-22 |publisher=[[Czech Radio]]|language=de}}</ref> but in 1983 she began publishing some short stories at Aufbau Verlagsgruppe in East-Berlin. Since then, her works are still published at Aufbau. In 2004, she cofounded a cultural institution for German-language writing in the greater Czech region, the {{ill|Prager Literaturhaus deutschsprachiger Autoren|de|Prager Literaturhaus deutschsprachiger Autoren}}, with František Černý und Kurt Krolop.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Kountouroyanis |first=Konstantin |date=2016-05-22 |title=Lenka Reinerová - Die Grande Dame der Prager Literatur wäre heute 100 |url=https://prag-aktuell.cz/blog/lenka-reinerova-die-grande-dame-der-prager-literatur-waere-heute-100-23052016-16932|access-date=2024-10-22 |website=prag-aktuell.cz|language=de}}</ref>
Reinerová had been largely a recluse ever since a spell in hospital in 2007, with the cause of her death not immediately known. Reinerová, the oldest living German-language writer in Prague, died on 27 June 2008 in Prague, in her apartment, at 92.<ref name="obit">{{cite news |url=http://www.lidovky.cz/zemrela-spisovatelka-lenka-reinerova-d8l-/ln_kultura.asp?c=A080627_183041_ln_kultura_mel |title=Zemřela spisovatelka Lenka Reinerová |newspaper=[[Lidové noviny]] |date=27 June 2008 |language=cs|accessdate=28 March 2016}}</ref> Lucie Černhousová, head of Literaturhaus, the Prague publisher of German-language writers, disclosed her death.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-06-27 |title=Nachruf: Lenka Reinerova starb mit 92 Jahren |trans-title=Lenka Reinerova dies at 92 years of age |url=https://www.tagesspiegel.de/kultur/literatur/nachruf-lenka-reinerova-starb-mit-92-jahren/1267180.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/2

title: Kurkulla
diff_added:
By 1902 Ada Evans had become Australia's first woman to graduate from the law school at the [[University of Sydney]], but after applying to the Full Court of the [[Supreme Court of New South Wales]] was refused admission to the [[bar (law)|bar]], as the law at that time disqualified woman from holding a position. After a determined legal battle, culminating in the ''Women's Legal Status Act 1918'' which was passed by the [[William Holman|Holman]] state government, she became the first woman in New South Wales to qualify for admission to the bar but then had to be a student of law for two years. Finally on the 12 May 1921 she became the first woman to be admitted to the bar in New South Wales, opening up the legal profession here to females for the first time.<ref name=nswshr-503/>
The [[Australian Law Journal]] of 20 May 1948 described Evans' struggle to gain admittance to the bar as "heroic pioneering for women in the profession".<ref name=nswshr-503/>
diff_removed:

Finally on the 12 May 1921 she became the first woman to be admitted to the Bar in New South Wales, opening up the legal profession here to females for the first time.<ref name=nswshr-503/>
The [[Australian Law Journal]] of 20 May 1948 described Evans' struggle to gain admittance to the Bar as "heroic pioneering for women in the profession".<ref name=nswshr-503/>

title: Publius Mummius Sisenna Rutilianus
diff_added: '''Publius Mummius Sisenna Rutilianus''' was a [[Roman Empire|Roman]] [[Roman Senate|senator]] of the second century AD. He is best known from [[Lucian]]'s vivid portrayal of him in ''Alexander vel Pseudomantis'', where the senator is described as "a man of good family and tested in many Roman offices, but utterly sick as far as the gods were concerned," as the most distinguished victim of the bogus oracle established by the story's namesake in [[Paphlagonia]]. Rutilianus was [[Roman consul|suffect consul]] in the ''[[nundinium]]'' of May-June 146 with [[Titus Prifernius Paetus Rosianus Geminus]] as his colleague.<ref>[[Géza Alföldy]], ''Konsulat und Senatorenstand unter den Antoninen'', (Bonn: Habelt Verlag, 1977), p. 151.</ref>

He is best known from [[Lucian]]'s vivid portrayal of him in ''Alexander vel Pseudomantis'', where the senator is described as "a man of good family and tested in many Roman offices, but utterly sick as far as the gods were concerned," as the most distinguished victim of the bogus oracle established by the story's namesake in [[Paphlagonia]]. title: Nicotine replacement therapy
diff_added: NRT products were first approved for use in the United States in 1984.<ref name=AHFS2015/> Nicotine replacement products are on the [[WHO Model List of Essential Medicines|World Health Organization's List of Essential Medicines]].<ref name="WHO21st" /> In 2024, the World Health Organization's first clinical tobacco-cessation guideline recommended nicotine replacement therapy as an effective treatment for tobacco cessation, and nicotine gum and transdermal patches became the first WHO-prequalified NRT products.<ref>{{Cite web |title=WHO releases first-ever clinical treatment guideline for tobacco cessation in adults |url=https://www.who.int/news/item/02-07-2024-who-releases-first-ever-clinical-treatment-guideline-for-tobacco-cessation-in-adults |access-date=2026-03-25 |website=www.who.int |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=WHO Launches First Global Guidelines for Quitting Tobacco |url=https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2822003 |access-date=2026-03-25 |website=jamanetwork.com}}</ref> They are available as [[generic medication]].<ref name=AHFS2015/>

title: Laura Dahlmeier
diff_added: On 28 July 2025, Dahlmeier died in a [[rockfall]] accident. She and her rope partner Marina Krauss were climbing [[Laila Peak (Hushe Valley)|Laila Peak]] in the [[Karakoram]] mountain range in [[Pakistan]].<ref name=":0" /> According to Krauss, who was waiting some distance below Dahlmeier at the time of the accident, the weather conditions at the nightly beginning of the ascend of the mountain were frosty and dry.<ref name="Schneider">{{Cite news|url=https://www.zdfheute.de/panorama/laura-dahlmeier-tot-seilpartnerin-krauss-100.html|title=Dahlmeiers Seilpartnerin berichtet: Halbe Stunde früher "wären wir sicher runtergekommen"|first=Tim-Julian|last=Schneider|date=31 July 2025|access-date=14 August 2025|language=de|trans-title=Dahlmeier's rope partner reports: Half an hour earlier, "we would have gotten down safely"|website=zdfheute.de}}</ref> As they climbed the mountain, the air temperature and humidity increased rapidly, and they decided to turn back before reaching the summit. On their way down, a rockfall occurred. Dahlmeier was hit on the head by a large rock and thrown against the face of the mountain, leaving her unresponsive and in a position where Krauss could not reach her.<ref name="Schneider"/><ref name=":0" /> She was killed instantly. Marina Krauss reported that she climbed to Laura to get a sky telephone and call for help, but saw that her helmet had been broken into pieces and her head was seriously injured. <ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.tagesschau.de/ausland/asien/dahlmeier-unglueck-pakistan-100.html|title=Was über das Dahlmeier-Unglück bekannt ist|date=30 July 2025|language=de|trans-title=What is known about the Dahlmeier accident|access-date=14 August 2025|website=tagesschau.de}}</ref>
diff_removed: On 28 July 2025, Dahlmeier died in a [[rockfall]] accident. She and her rope partner Marina Krauss were climbing [[Laila Peak (Hushe Valley)|Laila Peak]] in the [[Karakoram]] mountain range in [[Pakistan]].<ref name=":0" /> According to Krauss, who was waiting some distance below Dahlmeier at the time of the accident, the weather forecast for the day had been favorable.<ref name="Schneider">{{Cite news|url=https://www.zdfheute.de/panorama/laura-dahlmeier-tot-seilpartnerin-krauss-100.html|title=Dahlmeiers Seilpartnerin berichtet: Halbe Stunde früher "wären wir sicher runtergekommen"|first=Tim-Julian|last=Schneider|date=31 July 2025|access-date=14 August 2025|language=de|trans-title=Dahlmeier's rope partner reports: Half an hour earlier, "we would have gotten down safely"|website=zdfheute.de}}</ref> As they climbed the mountain, the air temperature and humidity increased rapidly, and they decided to turn back before reaching the summit. On their way down, a rockfall occurred. Dahlmeier was hit on the head by a large rock and thrown against the face of the mountain, leaving her unresponsive and in a position where Krauss could not reach her.<ref name="Schneider"/><ref name=":0" /> She was killed instantly. Marina Krauss reported that she climbed to Laura to get a sky telephone and call for help, but saw that her helmet had been broken into pieces and her head was seriously injured. <ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.tagesschau.de/ausland/asien/dahlmeier-unglueck-pakistan-100.html|title=Was über das Dahlmeier-Unglück bekannt ist|date=30 July 2025|language=de|trans-title=What is known about the Dahlmeier accident|access-date=14 August 2025|website=tagesschau.de}}</ref>

title: Type 051C destroyer
diff_removed:
The ship also possesses less advanced steam turbine propulsion compared to gas turbine propulsion present in all newer Chinese destroyers. The Type 051C is a stop gap measure to provide long range area air defense to the fleet by mating the mature hull design with proven SAM system that is more capable than that of the [[Type 052B destroyer]]. Production of this class stopped once more advanced [[Type 052C destroyer]]s became available.
The S-300FM missile is guided by the Volna (NATO codename: Tomb Stone) 3D phased-array target tracking radar installed on top of the stern deckhouse. The radar can direct 12 missiles to engage 6 targets simultaneously. Since two radars are required to give 360° coverage, this means that there is a blind gap on Type 051C in full SAM cover in the forward quadrant, resulting in a major tactical restriction.
There are two indigenous [[Type 347 Radar|Type 347G (EFR-1)]] I-band radars integrated with the Type 730 CIWS to provide fire-control.
*[[List of naval ship classes in service]]

title: Marcus Arruntius Aquila (consul 77)
diff_added: '''Marcus Arruntius Aquila''' was a [[Roman Empire|Roman]] [[Roman Senate|senator]] who flourished during the Flavian dynasty. Although he was a [[Roman consul|suffect consul]] for the ''[[nundinium]]'' of September-October 77 with [[Gaius Catellius Celer]], [[Ronald Syme]] notes that like the other consuls who came from Patavium (modern [[Padua]]), Aquila "failed to play a role in political life."<ref name=Syme-118>Syme, [https://www.jstor.org/stable/40310793 "Eight Consuls from Patavium"], ''Papers of the British School at Rome'', 51 (1983), p. 118</ref>

title: Jim Mutscheller
diff_added:
'''James 'Bucky' Mutscheller''' (March 31, 1930 – April 10, 2015) was an American professional [[American football|football]] player who split time at [[tight end]] and [[split end]] for nine seasons with the [[Baltimore Colts]] of the [[National Football League]] (NFL). He played [[college football]] for the [[Notre Dame Fighting Irish football|Notre Dame Fighting Irish]].
Mutscheller was born on March 31, 1930, in [[Beaver Falls, Pennsylvania]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Jim Mutscheller Stats, Height, Weight, Position, Draft, College |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/players/M/MutsJi00.htm |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=Pro-Football-Reference.com |language=en}}</ref> He played football, basketball and baseball at [[Beaver Falls High School]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Jim "Bucky" Mutscheller, Beaver County Sports Hall of Fame |url=https://www.bcshof.org/halloffamers/mutscheller1976.htm |access-date=2025-10-04 |website=www.bcshof.org}}</ref> In the first football game of the 1947 season, Mutscheller had a 49-yard [[touchdown]] [[Reception (gridiron football)|reception]] and 77-yard touchdown [[Carry (gridiron football)|run]] in a game against Erie East High School, played before 7,000 spectators.<ref>{{Cite news |date=September 6, 1947 |title=Beaver Falls Victor, 19–0, Jim Mutscheller Stars With 2 TDs |work=Pittsburgh Post-Gazette |pages=9}}</ref>
Mutscheller was commonly known as "Bucky" from the time he was in high school. In 1976, he was inducted into the Beaver County Sports Hall of Fame.<ref name=":0" />
Mutscheller was selected to play in the [[East–West Shrine Bowl|East-West Shrine Game]], but he suffered a knee injury in Notre Dame's final game of the season against the [[1951 USC Trojans football team|University of Southern California]] and had to be replaced.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Warden |first=Al |date=December 16, 1951 |title=Patrolling The Sports Highway, What Will Answer Be on Bowl Games? |work=The Ogden Standard-Examiner (Ogden, Utah) |pages=12A}}</ref> As a senior, the United Press named him second-team end on its All-Midwest team.<ref>{{Cite news |date=November 27, 1951 |title=Coleman Makes Midwest Team; Sugar on Second Squad |work=The Flint Journal (Flint, Michigan) |pages=9 |agency=United Press}}</ref>
Mutscheller signed with the Texans in June, 1952 but did not play for them in 1952 as he was in the Marines.<ref>{{Cite news |date=June 22, 1952 |title=Dallas Texans Add Pair Of Irish Aces |work=The Commercial Appeal (Memphis, Tennessee) |pages=2, Sec. III |agency=INS}}</ref> He was on the Texans' reserve list when Rosenbloom purchased the Texan assets. Mutscheller signed on to play for the Colts in February 1954, for $5,000 a year; joining the team after his military service ended at the beginning of August 1954. Future Hall of Fame Colts head coach [[Weeb Ewbank]] intended to use Mutscheller as a receiver.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Weeb Ewbank {{!}} Pro Football Hall of Fame |url=https://ww

title: Rio–Antirrio Bridge
diff_added: Due to the peculiar conditions of the straits, several unique engineering problems needed to be considered and overcome. The water depth reaches 65m, the seabed is mostly of loose sediment, the seismic activity and possibility of tectonic movement is significant, and the Gulf of Corinth is expanding at a rate of 10-15mm a year.<ref name="MSLM">{{cite journal|last1=Moretti|first1=Isabelle|last2=Sakellariou|first2=D|last3=Lykousis|first3=V|last4=Micarelli|first4=L|title=The Gulf of Corinth: an active half graben?|journal=Geodynamics|date=2003|volume=36|issue=1–2|pages=323–340|doi=10.1016/s0264-3707(03)00053-x|bibcode=2003JGeo...36..323M}}</ref> In addition, the hills on either side create a wind tunnel where {{convert|70|mph}} winds are common.

title: Fire (1996 film)
comment: /* Controversies and reaction */ Minor copy edit
diff_added: On 2 December, more than 200 [[Shiv Sena (1966–2022)|Shiv Sainiks]] stormed a Cinemax theatre in suburban [[Goregaon]] in [[Mumbai]], smashing glass panes, burning posters and shouting slogans.<ref name="NYT">{{Cite web|last=Bearak|first=Barry|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1998/12/24/world/new-delhi-journal-a-lesbian-idyll-and-the-movie-theaters-surrender.html|title=New Delhi Journal; A Lesbian Idyll, and the Movie Theaters Surrender|date=1998-12-24|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=2020-04-10|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241130183857/https://www.nytimes.com/1998/12/24/world/new-delhi-journal-a-lesbian-idyll-and-the-movie-theaters-surrender.html|archive-date=30 November 2024|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="IndiaToday"/> They compelled managers to refund tickets to moviegoers. On 3 December, the [[Regal Theatre, New Delhi|Regal theatre]] in [[Delhi]] was similarly stormed. American film critic [[William Arnold (film critic)|William Arnold]], in India to do a feature on the Bollywood Cinema, witnessed the Regal rampage and later reported: "I watched about a dozen [[Shiv Sena]] activists−including three women with canes−threaten the terrified manager, disperse the line of moviegoers, smash ticket booth and display windows, then force their way into the theater, smashing the food counter and terrorizing patrons. Outside, other Shiv Sena tore up the film's posters and billboards and set them ablaze in a bonfire." He added, "Apparently, this opening was particularly offensive to them because the Regal was a sacred site of Hindu supremacy: the spot where, 49 years ago, [[Mahatma Gandhi|Gandhi]]'s assassins−ex-members of the Shiv Sena's parent organization, the [[Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh|RSS]]−met on the fateful morning of the murder in January 1948."<ref>{{Cite news |last=Arnold |first=William |date=January 14, 1999 |title='Fire' Furor Spotlights Indian Dilemma |work=The Seattle Post-Intelligencer |pages=D3}}</ref>

American film critic [[William Arnold (film critic)|William Arnold]], in India to do a feature on the Bollywood Cinema, witnessed the Regal rampage: "I watched about a dozen [[Shiv Sena]] activists−including three women with canes−threaten the terrified manager, disperse the line of moviegoers, smash ticket booth and display windows, then force their way into the theater, smashing the food counter and terrorizing patrons. Outside, other Shiv Sena tore up the film's posters and billboards and set them ablaze in a bonfire."
